layout.metaDescription: AI-powered German learning platform with book reader, vocabulary, grammar, and scenarios
layout.aiDisclaimer: AI responses are generated by Gemini and may contain mistakes — always verify important German.

nav.shutdownBody: This stops the backend and frontend servers completely. Double-click DeutschPath.app to start again.

contact.tagline: Researcher by day · builder by night · learning German reluctantly
contact.bio: I made DeutschPath because I was too lazy to juggle five different apps just to learn one language. It turned into something I'm actually proud of. If you found a bug, have an idea, or just want to say the app helped you — I genuinely want to hear it.
contact.oneCoffee: One coffee = one less bug, or maybe one more feature I've been procrastinating on. 🤷
contact.sentBody: Your email client should be open. If it didn't pop up, send directly to {email}

feedback.label: Feedback
feedback.placeholder: What could be better? Even small things help — I read every message.

bug.label: Bug Report
bug.placeholder: Describe what happened and what you expected. Steps to reproduce = hero status.

collaboration.label: Collaboration
collaboration.placeholder: Tell me about the idea. I'm open to interesting things.

hi.label: Just saying hi
hi.placeholder: Go for it — no agenda needed.

contact.opensEmailClient: Opens your email client — no data leaves the app.
contact.footer: Made with ☕ & a bit of Prokrastination · Open source on GitHub

cases.title: German Cases (Fälle)
cases.subtitle: Complete reference for der/die/das in all four grammatical cases
cases.usage_Nominative_use: Subject of the sentence (who/what does the action)
cases.usage_Nominative_ex: Der Mann schläft. — The man sleeps.
cases.usage_Accusative_ex: Ich sehe den Mann. — I see the man.
cases.usage_Dative_ex: Ich gebe dem Mann das Buch. — I give the man the book.
cases.usage_Genitive_ex: Das Auto des Mannes. — The man's car.
cases.defArticlesFootnote: Note: Only masculine changes in Accusative (der → den). Feminine & Neuter are the same in Nom & Acc.
cases.indefArticlesFootnote: No plural indefinite article in German. Use 'keine' (negative) or just the noun.
cases.negArticlesFootnote: 'kein' follows the same pattern as 'ein' for singular; it also has a plural form unlike 'ein'.

keyChanges.label: Masculine Accusative:
keyChanges.text: der → den · ein → einen · kein → keinen

keyChanges.label: Feminine Dative:
keyChanges.text: der · einer · keiner (same as Masc Genitive!)

cases.adjIntro: Adjective endings depend on which article (if any) precedes them. There are three declension patterns.
cases.weakSubtitle: After definite articles: der / die / das / die
cases.mixedSubtitle: After indefinite/negative articles: ein / eine / kein / mein / dein / sein …
cases.strongSubtitle: No article before the adjective (adjective carries the gender information)
cases.patternTrickBody: The adjective ending mirrors the definite article ending when there's no article or only a weak article. Think of it as: someone has to show the gender — either the article or the adjective does. After der/die/das (which already shows gender clearly), adjectives mostly end in -e or -en.
cases.mnemonicAcc: Mnemonic: DOGFU — Durch, Ohne, Gegen, Für, Um
cases.mnemonicDat: Mnemonic: ABMNSSVZ — Aus, Bei, Mit, Nach, Seit, Von, Zu + außer, gegenüber, ab
cases.genSpokenNote: Note: In spoken German, many genitive prepositions are used with dative (wegen dem Wetter).
cases.twoWayRule: Ask wohin? (where to? → movement) → Accusative | Ask wo? (where? → location/state) → Dative
cases.possessiveNote: Possessives decline like kein (indefinite article pattern — mixed declension)
cases.relativeNote: Relative pronouns mirror definite articles, with a few exceptions in Dative plural and Genitive.
cases.relativeFootnote: Note: Dative plural is denen (not den), and Genitive uses dessen/deren.
cases.practiceIntro: Read the reference tables in the other tabs first, then test yourself here. All answers are checked case-insensitively. For adjective endings you can type with or without the leading <mono>-</mono> (e.g. <mono>en</mono> or <mono>-en</mono>).
cases.practice_definite_subtitle: Fill in the correct form of der / die / das
cases.practice_indefinite_subtitle: Fill in the correct form of ein / eine
cases.practice_negative_subtitle: Fill in the correct form of kein / keine
cases.practice_adj_weak_subtitle: After definite articles: der / die / das
cases.practice_adj_mixed_subtitle: After indefinite / negative articles: ein / kein
cases.fillAccDat: Fill in the Accusative and Dative forms
tips_derword: All der-words (dieser, jener, welcher, jeder, mancher) follow the SAME endings as definite articles
Masculine is the only gender that changes in Accusative: der → den / ein → einen
Feminine and Neuter are identical in Nominative and Accusative
tips_accusative: Only masculine articles change in accusative: der → den, ein → einen
Feminine stays die/eine, Neuter stays das/ein — same as nominative
This makes accusative much easier than it looks
tips_dative: Masculine and Neuter both use 'dem' in dative — remember: M+N → dem
Feminine dative is 'der' — same as masculine nominative! (context decides)
Plural dative: always 'den' + add -n to the noun (den Männern, den Kindern)
All adjectives end in -en in dative (without exception)
tips_genitive: Masculine and Neuter add -s (short words) or -es (longer words) to the noun
des Mannes, des Kindes — but: des Autos (already ends in -s-sound, add -s)
Feminine and Plural both use 'der' in genitive
In spoken German, von + Dative often replaces Genitive: das Buch von dem Mann
tips_prepositions: Accusative: DOGFU — Durch, Ohne, Gegen, Für, Um
Dative: ABMNSSVZ — Aus, Bei, Mit, Nach, Seit, Von, Zu + außer, gegenüber
Two-way prepositions: wohin? (where to) → Accusative | wo? (where) → Dative
Verbs of movement use Accusative; verbs of position (liegen, stehen, hängen) use Dative
tips_gender: Masculine (-er, -ling, -ist, -or): der Lehrer, der Frühling, der Tourist, der Motor
Neuter (-chen, -lein, -ment, -um): das Mädchen, das System, das Zentrum
All infinitives used as nouns are neuter: das Lesen, das Schreiben
